title: Structured Observability, Health, Profiling, Capacity, and Incidents · Concurrent Code Workshop
summary: A server pairing session predicts request, handler, dependency, response, and cleanup transitions before building the first low-cardinality service evidence and incident dashboard for structured observability, health, profiling, capacity, and incidents. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of low-cardinality service evidence and incident dashboard while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debug…
objectives: Emit one structured request completion record with request and trace IDs, method, route pattern, status, duration, bytes, trusted peer class, and redaction.
Measure duration distributions, active requests, request and response bytes, status classes, cancellations, rejects, saturation, and dependencies with bounded attributes.
Apply current OpenTelemetry server span kind, low-cardinality route, method, status, error.type, network and server attributes, propagation, sampling, and 4xx semantics.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Slog Request Record
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop team handles case 1221 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic Go evidence to use bounded route telemetry and distinguish server errors from readiness capacity; then drain the old revision while a canary fails its changed-case invariant. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Slog Request Record.

For Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Slog Request Record, keep Slog Request Record connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

Evidence target: Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Profile Capacity Incident.
steps.why: Retrieving Profile Capacity Incident through the concrete low-cardinality service evidence and incident dashboard case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Profile Capacity Incident, keep Profile Capacity Incident connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use benchmarks, pprof in a protected plane, traces, saturation, tail latency, allocation, contention, load shape, and changed-fault evidence for capacity and incidents.

hints: For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Profile Capacity Incident, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Profile Capacity Incident precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Profile Capacity Incident, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop team handles case 1230 in a civic incident API behind one trusted regional proxy. Build deterministic Go evidence to use bounded route telemetry and distinguish server errors from readiness capacity; then repeat the request with a different method and retain status plus durable-effect evidence. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use benchmarks, pprof in a protected plane, traces, saturation, tail latency, allocation, contention, load shape, and changed-fault evidence for capacity and incidents.

For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Profile Capacity Incident, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A lower microbenchmark nanoseconds-per-operation proves the deployed server can handle more user traffic.
